text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Metastatic Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer — ola Initiation Follow-Up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) harboring a germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant, here for his 6-week follow-up visit since initiating ola (Lynparza) following progression through abi/prednisone and then enza. Today's visit focuses on treatment tolerance, toxicity assessment, and early biochemical response evaluation.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski carries a well-established diagnosis of stage IV (T3b N1 M1b) Gleason 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5 prostatic adenocarcinoma, initially diagnosed approximately 3 years ago at age 68 with presenting PSA of 187 ng/mL, bone metastases involving the lumbar spine (L3, L4), right iliac wing, and right femoral neck, as well as a right obturator lymph node metastasis. Notably, germline testing at the time of diagnosis revealed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift mutation (c.5946delT, p.Ser1982Argfs*22), consistent with his family history of a BRCA2-associated pedigree (sister with breast cancer, brother with pancreatic cancer).

His initial treatment was ADT with Lupron plus abi acetate 1000 mg daily with prednisone 5 mg BID (mHSPC intensification), with an excellent biochemical response — PSA nadir of 0.8 ng/mL. Approximately 24 months into treatment, he developed biochemical progression fulfilling PCWG3 criteria (PSA rising over 3 consecutive measurements) with confirmed castrate testosterone, establishing castration-resistant disease. Restaging PSMA PET/CT at that time demonstrated new osseous metastatic lesions at T10, right posterior 7th rib, left posterior 9th rib, and right sacral ala, with progressive right obturator nodal involvement.

He was transitioned to enza 160 mg daily with continuation of leup and addition of denosumab 120 mg subcutaneous monthly for bone protection. He achieved a PSA nadir of 0.6 ng/mL on enza with improvement in bone pain and stable radiographic disease at 5-month restaging. However, at approximately 6 months, PSA began rising again — reaching 8.3 ng/mL — and repeat PSMA PET/CT documented marked disease progression with numerous new osseous metastases, enlargement of the right obturator lymph node to 2.1 cm, and new lesions at the right femoral head/neck, bilateral iliac crests, L1-L2 vertebral bodies, sternum, and multiple additional ribs.

Prior to starting the next line of therapy, a CT-guided bone biopsy of a right iliac crest lesion was performed, confirming metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma, Grade Group 5, with no neuroendocrine transdifferentiation (synaptophysin negative, chromogranin negative), AR positive, Ki-67 35%, and BRCA2 homozygous deletion confirmed by FISH — confirming biallelic BRCA2 loss. Given his germline BRCA2 pathogenic variant with somatic second hit and prior documentation on somatic NGS showing BRCA2 loss of heterozygosity at VAF 78%, he was initiated on Lynparza 300 mg orally twice daily per FDA approval for BRCA1/2-mutated mCRPC (consistent with PROfound trial data), approximately 6 weeks ago. ADT (leup) and denosumab were continued. Baseline PSA at ola initiation was 8.3 ng/mL; baseline CBC showed Hgb 11.8 g/dL, platelets 198 K/uL, WBC 6.1 K/uL.

Today the patient presents for his first formal follow-up since starting ola. Overall, he reports that treatment has been manageable. He notes significant fatigue, which he rates as moderately bothersome but acknowledges has been somewhat present since enza. He also reports nausea, particularly in the first 2-3 weeks that has since improved substantially with the anti-emetics prescribed (ondansetron 8 mg as needed). His right hip pain, which was notable at the time of progression on enza (Grade 2), has improved and he now rates it as mild (Grade 1), requiring only occasional acetaminophen.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fatigue present, Grade 2 in severity — most notable in the afternoon. No fever or chills. No night sweats. No significant unintentional weight loss; weight stable compared to last visit.
Gastrointestinal: Nausea improved (was Grade 1-2 in weeks 1-3, now Grade 1, well-controlled with ondansetron PRN). No vomiting. No diarrhea. Appetite mildly reduced but adequate oral intake maintained.
Musculoskeletal: Right hip pain improved to Grade 1. Back pain at lumbar spine (L3-L4) region remains mild (Grade 1), stable. No new bone pain reported.
Neurologic: No headache, no dizziness, no falls since the last fall event 3-4 months ago on enza. No seizures. He notes mild cognitive slowness that he attributes to lingering enza effects versus the new regimen — difficult to disentangle.
Respiratory: No shortness of breath at rest. Mild dyspnea on significant exertion, which he attributes to deconditioning and anemia. No cough, no hemoptysis.
Genitourinary: No hematuria. Urinary function at baseline — mild obstructive symptoms unchanged from prior. Continues on tamsulosin.
Endocrine: Hot flashes ongoing, consistent with ADT — manageable.
Hematologic: No bleeding events. No easy bruising beyond baseline.
Psychosocial: Patient reports mood is stable. He and his family are coping but expressed hopefulness regarding the PSA response seen on today's labs (reviewed below).

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
(As this is a follow-up note within the same oncology practice, this section summarizes key active problems)
- Metastatic CRPC with BRCA2 germline pathogenic variant (primary diagnosis, see above)
- Hypertension — on amlodipine 10 mg and lisinopril 10 mg; required dose adjustment early in disease course due to Zytiga-related hypertension
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well-controlled on metformin 1000 mg BID; HbA1c last checked approximately 6 months ago at 6.8%
- Hyperlipidemia — on atorvastatin 40 mg
- Osteoporosis/bone metastases — on denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg SC monthly with calcium/vitamin D supplementation

SOCIAL HISTORY
Retired steelworker. Non-smoker. Occasional alcohol prior to diagnosis, now abstains. Lives with his wife. Two adult children involved in care. Good social support structure. Eastern European descent.

FAMILY HISTORY
Brother: pancreatic cancer (deceased)
Sister: breast cancer (BRCA2-associated pedigree)
Parents: information limited; mother had unknown malignancy in her 70s

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS
1. Lynparza (Lynparza) 300 mg PO BID (started approximately 6 weeks ago)
2. Lupron acetate (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg IM every 3 months (ongoing ADT)
3. Denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg SC every 4 weeks
4. Amlodipine 10 mg PO daily
5. Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily
6. Metformin 1000 mg PO BID
7. Atorvastatin 40 mg PO nightly
8. Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO nightly
9. Calcium carbonate 1250 mg PO BID (with vitamin D3 1000 IU)
10. Acetaminophen 500–1000 mg PO PRN (bone pain)
11. Ondansetron 8 mg PO PRN (nausea)
12. Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily (GI prophylaxis)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 74 bpm, RR 16, Temperature 98.1°F, SpO2 96% on room air, Weight 178 lbs (down approximately 2 lbs from last visit).

General: Alert and oriented x3. Appears mildly fatigued but in no acute distress. Well-nourished. Ambulatory without assistance.
HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Mucous membranes moist. Conjunctivae mildly pale, consistent with anemia noted on CBC.
Neck: No cervical lymphadenopathy. No supraclavicular adenopathy.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly appreciated on palpation. No masses. Bowel sounds present.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No lower extremity asymmetry. Right hip tenderness on deep palpation with active range of motion at end range — mild. No skin breakdown.
Neurologic: Alert, oriented, conversational. Gait steady without assistance. No focal deficits.
Skin: No rash or skin breakdown. No pallor of the palms beyond mild conjunctival pallor.
Lymph Nodes: No peripheral lymphadenopathy on palpation.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (today)

Complete Blood Count:
  - Hemoglobin: 10.2 g/dL (baseline at Lynparza start 11.8 g/dL — decline of 1.6 g/dL)
  - Hematocrit: 31.4%
  - MCV: 85 fL
  - Platelets: 176 K/uL (baseline 198 K/uL — mild decline, within normal limits)
  - WBC: 5.3 K/uL (baseline 6.1 K/uL)
  - Neutrophils: 3.1 K/uL (ANC adequate)
  - Reticulocyte count: pending

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL (eGFR ~62 mL/min/1.73m2, stable)
  - BUN: 18 mg/dL
  - Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.2 mEq/L
  - AST: 28 U/L, ALT: 24 U/L (within normal limits)
  - Alkaline phosphatase: 148 U/L (mildly elevated, consistent with osseous metastatic disease — slightly improved from prior)
  - Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL
  - Albumin: 3.7 g/dL

PSA: 4.1 ng/mL (decline from 8.3 ng/mL at Lynparza initiation — approximately 51% decline)
Testosterone: < 20 ng/dL (confirmed castrate)
Chromogranin A: not drawn today
LDH: 198 U/L (borderline, not significantly elevated)

---

IMAGING

No new imaging obtained at today's visit. Formal restaging scan anticipated at the 5-6 month mark. Most recent imaging (PSMA PET/CT at time of enza progression, approximately 6-7 weeks ago) showed widespread osseous metastatic disease with new lesions at the right femoral head/neck, bilateral iliac crests, L1-L2, sternum, and multiple ribs, as well as right obturator lymph node enlargement at 2.1 cm — this was the basis for transitioning to ola.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with heavily pre-treated mCRPC — having progressed through ADT plus abi in the mHSPC setting, followed by Xtandi in the CRPC setting — now 6 weeks into PARP inhibitor therapy with Lynparza 300 mg BID, selected on the basis of his confirmed biallelic BRCA2 loss (germline c.5946delT pathogenic variant with somatic second hit by LOH demonstrated on both NGS and FISH on bone biopsy).

1. ola RESPONSE — ENCOURAGING EARLY BIOCHEMICAL DECLINE
PSA has fallen from 8.3 ng/mL to 4.1 ng/mL, a 51% decline at 6 weeks. This is consistent with the magnitude and kinetics of PSA responses seen in BRCA2-mutated patients in PROfound and related trials, where patients with BRCA1/2 alterations demonstrated the most robust benefits from ola. This is promising and we will continue full-dose Lynparza 300 mg BID. The patient and family were encouraged by this result. Right hip pain has improved, which may also reflect early tumor response at the femoral metastasis, though this is difficult to attribute with certainty at this early stage.

2. ANEMIA — EARLY Lynparza-RELATED MYELOSUPPRESSION
Hemoglobin has dropped from 11.8 g/dL at baseline to 10.2 g/dL today — a decline of 1.6 g/dL over 6 weeks. This is a Grade 1 anemia by CTCAE criteria at this hemoglobin level, though it warrants close monitoring. Anemia is one of the most common and clinically significant adverse effects of Lynparza, occurring in approximately 20-25% of patients in pivotal trials, often reaching Grade 3 severity over time. The patient's baseline anemia risk is further compounded by his chronic disease state, prior androgen deprivation, and prior ADT-related erythropoietin suppression.
  - Reticulocyte count is pending; will review.
  - Iron studies will be sent today to establish baseline and identify any treatable contributing iron deficiency.
  - Plan: CBC to be repeated in 3 weeks and again at the 3-month mark. No dose modification at this time — anemia is Grade 1 and patient is asymptomatic from a hematologic standpoint. Will counsel the patient to report worsening dyspnea, chest pain, lightheadedness, or palpitations immediately.
  - Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents are not recommended given active malignancy context and associated thromboembolic risk.

3. NAUSEA AND FATIGUE — MANAGEABLE TOXICITY
Gastrointestinal side effects (nausea) have improved substantially with scheduled/PRN ondansetron and taking Lynparza with food. Ongoing nausea Grade 1, no dose modification needed. Fatigue is Grade 2, which is significant but not uncommon in the context of ola plus the cumulative burden of prolonged ADT, progressive bone disease, and underlying anemia. No dose reduction is warranted at this time.
  - Discussed fatigue management strategies: pacing activity, prioritizing rest, light physical activity as tolerated.
  - Physical therapy referral from prior fall workup on Xtandi remains active — encouraged him to attend scheduled sessions.

4. BONE HEALTH AND PAIN MANAGEMENT
Denosumab 120 mg SC monthly is ongoing. Next dose due in approximately 2 weeks — reminder given to patient/wife. Dental hygiene reviewed — no planned dental procedures in the near future, which is appropriate given denosumab use and osteonecrosis of the jaw risk (though ONJ risk over years of use is notable and patient has been counseled previously). Calcium and vitamin D supplementation continued.
Right hip pain (right femoral head/neck lesion) is improving and currently Grade 1. No concern for impending pathologic fracture at this time, but if pain worsens or function declines we would obtain orthopedic surgery and/or radiation oncology consultation for prophylactic fixation versus palliative radiation. Acetaminophen continued PRN.

5. ONGOING ADT
leup acetate continued at 22.5 mg IM every 3 months — next injection due in approximately 6 weeks. Testosterone confirmed castrate at < 20 ng/dL — confirming continued AR-axis suppression is maintained alongside ola therapy. Continuation of ADT alongside PARP inhibitor therapy is standard practice in mCRPC per current guidelines and trial protocols.

6. COMORBIDITY MANAGEMENT
Hypertension: BP 138/82 today — acceptable control. Amlodipine and lisinopril continued. Will monitor closely.
Type 2 diabetes: Patient reports good dietary compliance. Metformin continued. Will defer detailed DM management to PCP; HbA1c check recommended at next PCP visit.
Hyperlipidemia: Atorvastatin continued.

7. GOALS OF CARE AND PATIENT EDUCATION
The early PSA response was reviewed with the patient and his wife, who were encouraged. We discussed that while this is a promising early signal, our goals of therapy remain palliative — focused on disease control, quality of life, and prolonging meaningful function. We discussed the typical trajectory of PARP inhibitor therapy in BRCA2-mutated mCRPC — response durations in PROfound ranged from months to over a year for excellent responders with BRCA1/2 mutations, but eventual resistance is anticipated. We have noted that his NGS profile at initial biopsy already showed co-occurring TP53 mutation, PTEN deletion, and RB1 heterozygous loss — markers of genomic complexity that may be relevant to response duration and future resistance.
The patient reaffirmed his desire to continue active treatment and his understanding that treatment is palliative in intent. He deferred hospice/palliative care integration discussion for now, but this will be revisited at each visit.

FOLLOW-UP:
- Return to clinic in 3-4 weeks for CBC recheck and symptom assessment.
- Labs at next visit: CBC with differential, CMP, PSA, testosterone, iron studies review.
- Formal restaging imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis or bone scan) to be scheduled at the 5-month mark on ola.
- Continue ola 300 mg PO BID at this time — no dose modification.
- Patient instructed to call the oncology triage line promptly if he develops worsening shortness of breath, fever, significant worsening of bone pain, or any new symptoms.

Visit time approximately 45 minutes; greater than half of time spent in counseling and care coordination.

Electronically signed,
Attending Oncologist, Hematology/Oncology
